authors: Not explicitly listed in provided text
01a_quotes.title: AI Copilots in Real Estate: Evidence from China

study_design: Our primary design is a difference-in-differences (DiD) that exploits cross-district variation in pre-existing app penetration.
To validate our findings and relax the DiD's identifying assumptions, we complement this analysis with a field experiment in Jinan, which randomized AI access across neighborhoods.
We estimate the effect using a continuous diff-in-diffs regression
We test for parallel pre-trends by estimating a flexible diff-in-diffs event-study specification
The partner firm ran the experiment in Jinan (a city outside our main sample) during 2019, randomizing AI copilot access at the neighborhood level.
In treated neighborhoods, the copilot was available in the agent app; in control neighborhoods, it was disabled (agents were told the feature was under maintenance).
Half (34) of the 68 neighborhoods in Jinan were randomized to the control group.
The experiment ran for roughly eleven months through the end of 2019.
task: Embedded directly in the agent-client chat workflow, the copilot advises agents on four tasks: for buyers, it recommends suitable properties and reply scripts; for sellers, it suggests price adjustments and property titles and descriptions.
For buyer-facing workflow, the copilot recommends to agents suitable listings based on conversation text and buyers' browsing histories
Additionally, the AI suggests professional reply scripts to improve communication
For seller-facing workflow, the copilot suggests dynamic price adjustments using comparable listings and market information
proposes optimized titles and descriptions to highlight key selling points

The typical transaction process on the platform is summarized in Figure A1. Buyers initiate in-person or via the app. The agent elicits preferences, recommends listings, schedules on-site viewings, and brokers negotiation. A seller lists through an agent in-person or via the app. The agent helps set an initial price, writes titles and descriptions, monitors interest, and recommends price adjustments.
population: We use a proprietary dataset from 18 cities for roughly one year before and after the copilot's introduction.
This sample includes about 230,000 completed transactions, for which we observe final prices and time on market for buyers and sellers.
A subset of 90,000 transactions are rated, and for these transactions we observe overall ratings, review text, and structured feedback labels.
The data contains a weekly panel of over 13 million listings
The transaction sample contains roughly 230,000 closings; we drop around 20 observations for missing fields.

For a subset of roughly one-third of these closings, we also observe post-transaction feedback from both buyers and sellers, comprising 5-point overall ratings, text reviews, and structured labels to evaluate specific aspects of agent's service
These post-transaction feedback entries include 75,596 from buyers and 20,336 from sellers.
By 2019 it operated thousands of offline stores across more than one hundred cities and employed on the order of one hundred thousand agents, facilitating millions of annual transactions.
The field experiment in Jinan involved 68 neighborhoods total.
arms: Our treatment intensity is based on pre-treatment app penetration provided by the firm at the district level: the share of transactions conducted through the app, and separately, the buyer- and seller-side shares.
In treated neighborhoods, the copilot was available in the agent app; in control neighborhoods, it was disabled (agents were told the feature was under maintenance).
Half (34) of the 68 neighborhoods in Jinan were randomized to the control group.
The AI copilot was introduced in 16 January 2019, roughly at the mid-point of our window.
outcome_definitions: At the listings level, our main conversion outcome is an indicator for sale within 3 months of listing.
For listings that successfully sell, we observe a transaction record (closing date and price, last listed price, and time-on-market for buyers and sellers)

Our dependent variables, yi,n,d,c,t, are whether the listed property is sold within three months, the time on market for sellers, time on market for buyers, and housing transaction prices for transaction i in neighborhood n district d city c in year-month t.
We use the natural logarithm of one plus days on market for sellers, days on market for buyers and transaction prices as our dependent variables.

For a subset of roughly one-third of these closings, we also observe post-transaction feedback from both buyers and sellers, comprising 5-point overall ratings, text reviews, and structured labels to evaluate specific aspects of agent's service (e.g., "accurate recommendation", "trustworthy", "reasonable price adjustment", "selling-point accuracy")

We define the conversion outcome this way to handle the usual truncation issue as some listings enter before or exit after our window. We chose 3 months because 54% of properties that are eventually sold are sold within 3 months.
statistical_results: The mean of Log days seller is about 3.99 (or 55 days on market for sellers).
The mean of Log days buyer is about 2.66, corresponding to an average time on market of about 15 days for buyers.
The mean of Log price is 10.27, corresponding to an average transaction price of 28,800 RMB per square meter, or approximately 4,122 USD per square meter.

for a district at mean pre-treatment app penetration (0.446), the likelihood that a listed property is sold within the first three months after it is listed increases by 18.8 percentage points (0.4215*0.446) compared to the control group (agents without access to AI).
the time on market for sellers is lower by 0.198 (–0.4434*0.446) compared to the control group. Relative to a baseline pre-treatment mean of 3.757, this represents a 5.27% decrease.
access to AI copilots reduces the time on market for buyers by 0.111 (-0.2484*0.446), or by 4.04% relative to the baseline mean of 2.747.
The estimated coefficient indicates an increase in transaction prices by 0.045 (0.10 ×0.446), relative to the control group, a 0.43% increase from the pre-treatment mean of 10.535.
buyer ratings rise by 9.84% (0.2206 × 0.446), and ratings from sellers by 15.49% (0.3474 × 0.446).

By late 2019, about 60% of AI recommendations were adopted by agents.

Given that the average unit price of housing in our sample is 47,351 RMB and the average building size is 84.28 square meters, the adoption of AI copilot leads to an average increased in price by about 17,160 RMB, or about 2,400 USD (0.43% * 47,351 RMB * 84.28).
ambiguity_or_caveats: We do not observe function-by-function tool usage. These pre-period shares are proxies for differential exposure because the copilot's buyer- (seller-) facing features live in the agent–buyer (agent–seller) chat flows.
We do not attempt to estimate the full surplus split. Instead, we ask whether buyers appear worse off.

While we acknowledge these possibilities, the institutional context and the pattern of our results suggest that any such spillovers are likely limited in scope. To the extent that some rapid learning does occur and benefit the control group, our results should be interpreted as a conservative lower bound of the AI's true impact on market efficiency.

An agent could learn general principles from their AI-assisted interactions over time and apply this knowledge to their offline clients. While this form of learning is possible, the event study graphs (e.g., in Figures 2 and 3) show an immediate and sharp effect in the first month following the AI's introduction, suggesting that learning over time is not the primary driver of our results.

Our study is subject to several limitations. First, our post period covers about one year, so we may not capture longer-run equilibrium adjustments as agents and clients adapt and as the platform iterates on design.

Second, our welfare evidence relies on ratings and search measures rather than direct surplus estimates. A more direct quantification of consumer surplus remains a valuable avenue for future research.

Third, external validity also merits discussion. Our setting features firm-employed agents who often intermediate both sides of a transaction, which differs from single-sided representation common in some other countries, e.g., the United States.

We expect the efficiency and "leveling" effect to generalize broadly, as these address fundamental issues of reducing information friction and augmenting professional skills that exist in any market. However, the net price effect is likely context-specific and depends on relative adoption and effectiveness of buyer- versus seller-facing tools, as well as institutional details that shape agent incentives.
While we acknowledge these possibilities, the institutional context and the pattern of our results suggest that any such spillovers are likely limited in scope.
Importantly, AI recommendations are advisory and visible only to agents, who retain full discretion over adoption decisions without managerial pressure.
We do not observe function-level usage; instead, "exposure" to buyer- vs. seller-facing workflows is proxied by pre-period district shares of buyers and sellers transacting via the app
While the evidence points to cross-side effects, we do not find strong evidence on feedback loop.
We emphasize that these are suggestive surplus inferences, consistent across feedback, behavior, and decomposition, but not a full decomposition surplus redistribution.

We use the field experiment as corroborative evidence for three reasons: the single-city scope and smaller sample, the lack of buyer– and seller–facing exposure proxies, and the absence of weekly behavior panels in this dataset.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study of an AI copilot tool introduced on a Chinese real estate platform. The paper uses a difference-in-differences design exploiting variation in pre-treatment app penetration across districts, complemented by a randomized field experiment in Jinan. The main outcomes are: (1) probability of sale within 3 months, (2) time-on-market for buyers and sellers, (3) transaction prices, and (4) post-transaction ratings. The paper decomposes effects into buyer-facing and seller-facing workflows to study cross-side effects. Key findings include efficiency gains (faster sales, shorter time-on-market), price increases driven by buyer-side exposure, and higher satisfaction ratings on both sides. Heterogeneity analysis shows larger effects for less-experienced agents and in thinner markets.

01_extraction.title: AI Copilots in Real Estate: Evidence from China
01_extraction.study_design_description: The study employs a difference-in-differences (DiD) design exploiting cross-district variation in pre-existing app penetration, estimated using a continuous diff-in-diffs regression with a flexible event-study specification to test for parallel pre-trends. This is complemented by a field experiment in Jinan that randomized AI copilot access at the neighborhood level, with half (34) of 68 neighborhoods assigned to control and half to treatment. The experiment ran for roughly eleven months through the end of 2019, with the AI copilot introduced on 16 January 2019.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: The AI copilot is embedded in the agent-client chat workflow and advises agents on four tasks: for buyers, it recommends suitable properties based on conversation text and browsing histories, and suggests professional reply scripts to improve communication; for sellers, it suggests dynamic price adjustments using comparable listings and market information, and proposes optimized titles and descriptions to highlight key selling points.
01_extraction.population_description: The main analysis uses a proprietary dataset from 18 cities covering roughly one year before and after the copilot's introduction, including about 230,000 completed transactions with observed final prices and time on market. A subset of 90,000 transactions are rated, with 75,596 buyer ratings and 20,336 seller ratings. The data contains a weekly panel of over 13 million listings. The field experiment in Jinan involved 68 neighborhoods total.

arms.description: In treated neighborhoods, the copilot was available in the agent app. Treatment intensity in the DiD is based on pre-treatment app penetration at the district level (share of transactions conducted through the app).
In control neighborhoods, the copilot was disabled (agents were told the feature was under maintenance).

outcomes.outcome_description: An indicator for whether the listed property is sold within three months of listing. Chosen because 54% of properties that are eventually sold are sold within 3 months.
Natural logarithm of one plus days on market for sellers
Natural logarithm of one plus days on market for buyers
Post-transaction overall rating from buyers, comprising 5-point overall ratings to evaluate agent's service
Post-transaction overall rating from sellers, comprising 5-point overall ratings to evaluate agent's service
Structured feedback label evaluating whether agent provided accurate recommendations
Structured feedback label evaluating talk inefficiency
Structured feedback label evaluating talk comfort
Structured feedback label evaluating whether pricing was optimal
Structured feedback label evaluating accuracy of selling-point descriptions

01_extraction.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study of an AI copilot tool introduced on a Chinese real estate platform on 16 January 2019. The main DiD exploits cross-district variation in pre-treatment app penetration (continuous treatment intensity). A complementary randomized field experiment in Jinan randomized AI access at the neighborhood level (34 treated, 34 control neighborhoods). The paper decomposes effects into buyer-facing and seller-facing workflows and examines heterogeneity by agent tenure. Key findings: AI access increases probability of sale within 3 months, reduces time-on-market for both buyers and sellers, increases transaction prices (driven by buyer-side exposure), and improves satisfaction ratings. Effects are larger for less-experienced agents. The paper notes that AI recommendations are advisory and visible only to agents, who retain full discretion. The post-period covers about one year, so longer-run equilibrium effects are not captured. Adoption rate by late 2019 was about 60% of AI recommendations. The paper reports that adoption led to an average price increase of about 17,160 RMB (approximately 2,400 USD) per transaction.

02_method.rationale: The study employs a difference-in-differences (DiD) design exploiting cross-district variation in pre-existing app penetration, with a flexible event-study specification to test for parallel pre-trends. While a field experiment is also mentioned, the main analysis uses the quasi-experimental DiD approach across 18 cities, making natural_experiment the primary classification.

per_outcome.operationalization: Binary indicator of whether the property sold within 3 months of listing, measured from transaction records
Natural logarithm of (1 + days on market) for seller transactions, measured from listing to sale date
Natural logarithm of (1 + days on market) for buyer transactions, measured from search start to purchase
Natural logarithm of final transaction price in RMB from completed sales records
Post-transaction 5-point overall rating of agent service quality provided by buyers
Post-transaction 5-point overall rating of agent service quality provided by sellers
Binary structured feedback label indicating whether agent provided accurate property recommendations
Continuous structured feedback measure of communication inefficiency in agent-client conversations
Continuous structured feedback measure of client comfort during agent conversations
Continuous structured feedback measure of client-perceived trustworthiness of the agent
Count of on-site property viewings arranged, measured from scheduling records
Natural logarithm of initial listing price in RMB when property first posted
Binary structured feedback label indicating whether the pricing strategy was optimal
Binary structured feedback label indicating accuracy of property selling-point descriptions
Weekly count of price adjustment actions taken by sellers, measured from listing modification logs
Average magnitude of price changes across all adjustments, measured in continuous units

04_onet.rationale: The participants are real estate agents working with both buyers and sellers in property transactions. The paper explicitly describes their work: communicating with clients via chat, recommending properties to buyers, negotiating prices for sellers, and managing listings. These are the core responsibilities of Real Estate Sales Agents (41-9022.00), whose work encompasses facilitating property transactions, advising clients, and managing property information. The AI copilot augments their entire workflow rather than a single narrow task, making the occupation-level mapping most appropriate.

alternates.onet_label: Providing Consultation and Advice to Others

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm present: ai_copilot_access, which provides the copilot feature to agents. Control arm has the copilot disabled.
llm_extracted.notes: This is a DiD design with treatment intensity based on pre-treatment app penetration (transaction ratio = 0.446). The primary statistic uses the DiD coefficient Post×Ratio_tran = -0.4434 (SE 0.145). The outcome is log-transformed (log of 1 + days on market), so the coefficient directly represents the log ratio. The paper reports the effect as -0.198 log-points at mean treatment intensity (=-0.4434*0.446), which translates to a 5.27% decrease relative to baseline mean of 3.757. Since the outcome is already log-transformed, the regression coefficient can be used directly as the log ratio. Multiple specifications are reported (field experiment in Jinan, buyer-facing vs seller-facing exposure, heterogeneity by tenure), but the main DiD with transaction ratio is the primary specification for the overall treatment effect.
source_quotes: the time on market for sellers is lower by 0.198 (−0.4434*0.446) compared to the control group. Relative to a baseline pre-treatment mean of 3.757, this represents a 5.27% decrease.

computed.notes: This is a DiD design with treatment intensity based on pre-treatment app penetration (transaction ratio = 0.446). The primary statistic uses the DiD coefficient Post×Ratio_tran = -0.4434 (SE 0.145). The outcome is log-transformed (log of 1 + days on market), so the coefficient directly represents the log ratio. The paper reports the effect as -0.198 log-points at mean treatment intensity (=-0.4434*0.446), which translates to a 5.27% decrease relative to baseline mean of 3.757. Since the outcome is already log-transformed, the regression coefficient can be used directly as the log ratio. Multiple specifications are reported (field experiment in Jinan, buyer-facing vs seller-facing exposure, heterogeneity by tenure), but the main DiD with transaction ratio is the primary specification for the overall treatment effect. | CI missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Field experiment in Jinan randomized AI copilot access at the neighborhood level. Treatment group (n=34 neighborhoods) had AI copilot enabled; control group (n=34 neighborhoods) had it disabled. This is the cleanest experimental comparison in the paper.
llm_extracted.notes: The field experiment (Table 6, column 6) reports Post×Treat coefficient = 0.2323 with SE = 0.096 for Rating (seller). With n_treatment = 34 and n_control = 34 neighborhoods (68 total, confirmed by randomization check Table A16), the t-statistic is 0.2323/0.096 = 2.42 with df = 68-2 = 66. The paper reports p < 0.01 (inequality only, not exact p-value). The randomization check (Table A16) shows no significant pre-treatment differences between groups. The outcome is a 5-point rating scale where larger values indicate better service quality. Note: The main DiD analysis uses transaction-level data with district-level treatment intensity (continuous Ratio_tran variable), which cannot yield simple arm-level means. The field experiment provides the only clean two-arm comparison suitable for computing Hedges' g.
source_quotes: Table 6: Field Experiment: Intent-to-Treat Effects of Copilot Access ... Post×Treat 0.2323** (0.096) ... Rating (seller)
The experiment ran for roughly eleven months through the end of 2019. Randomization checks show no meaningful pre-treatment differences across conditions (Appendix Table A16).
Half (34) of the 68 neighborhoods in Jinan were randomized to the control group.
Table A16: Randomization Check ... Rating (seller) Treatment Group 4.7232 Control Group 4.7431 p-Value of T-Test 0.78
Standard errors clustered at the neighborhood level are reported in parentheses. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1

computed.notes: The field experiment (Table 6, column 6) reports Post×Treat coefficient = 0.2323 with SE = 0.096 for Rating (seller). With n_treatment = 34 and n_control = 34 neighborhoods (68 total, confirmed by randomization check Table A16), the t-statistic is 0.2323/0.096 = 2.42 with df = 68-2 = 66. The paper reports p < 0.01 (inequality only, not exact p-value). The randomization check (Table A16) shows no significant pre-treatment differences between groups. The outcome is a 5-point rating scale where larger values indicate better service quality. Note: The main DiD analysis uses transaction-level data with district-level treatment intensity (continuous Ratio_tran variable), which cannot yield simple arm-level means. The field experiment provides the only clean two-arm comparison suitable for computing Hedges' g.
